🔌 Proxy Setup
🖥 Test report builder
📂 Report Viewer
☁️ OAuth Proxy
🔌 Setup Guide

Proxy Companion — Quick Start

Intercept Postman traffic live in TestReport Builder via the local MITM proxy companion.

1
Start Companion
2
Open Settings
3
Disable SSL
4
Set Proxy
5
Connect
6
CA cert
7
Send requests
1
Start the Companion
Terminal

Open PowerShell (or any terminal) in the folder containing companion.exe and run:

companion.exe

The companion prints the ports it listens on — keep this window open:

TestReport Builder — Proxy Companion

Proxy  →  http://localhost:8888  (Postman setting)
API    →  http://localhost:8889
CA cert →  http://localhost:8889/ca.pem
⚠ Upstream TLS not verified (rejectUnauthorized)
Waiting for requests…
[API] Control server running on :8889
Proxy port → localhost:8888
API port   → localhost:8889

Note both ports — you will need them in the following steps.

2
Open Postman Settings
Postman

Click the ⚙ gear icon (top-right) → App settings.

3
Disable SSL certificate verification
Postman → General

Go to General → scroll to SSL certificate verification and turn it OFF.

The companion acts as a MITM proxy with a self-signed CA — Postman would otherwise reject intercepted HTTPS connections.

4
Set custom proxy
Postman → Proxy

In Settings → Proxy, enable Use custom proxy configuration and enter the proxy port from Step 1:

  • a
    Protocol: http
  • b
    Host: localhost
  • c
    Port: 8888 — proxy port shown by the companion
  • d
    Check both HTTP and HTTPS
5
Open TestReport Builder and click Proxy
Builder

Open testreport-builder.html in Chrome or Edge. Click the ● Proxy button in the sidebar to expand the connection panel.

6
Connect to the companion
Builder → Proxy panel

The panel shows the API port (8889 by default — from Step 1). Verify it matches, then click Connect.

The dot turns green — Connected — listening.

💡 If you see "Companion not running" — make sure companion.exe is still running in your terminal (Step 1).

7
Install the CA certificate in Postman
Postman — optional
🟢

Optional — skip if SSL verification is OFF (Step 3). Only needed if you want to keep SSL verification enabled in Postman and still use the proxy.

While connected, click CA.pem in the Proxy panel to download the certificate. Then in Postman go to Settings → Certificates and add it as a trusted CA.

8
Send requests in Postman — see them appear in Builder
Done ✓

Everything is wired up. Use Postman normally:

  • 1
    Send any HTTP / HTTPS request in Postman.
  • 2
    The companion intercepts it and streams the captured test case to the Builder via SSE.
  • 3
    The request appears instantly in the Builder sidebar — method, path, status code, and response body all pre-filled.
  • 4
    Review and edit the captured cases, then save as an execution file and Generate Report — a standalone HTML test report of the execution.
💡

Use Clear in the Proxy panel to discard captured requests and start fresh. Use Disconnect to stop capturing without closing the companion.

📦 Installation Guide

Environment Setup

Install required tools — once per machine.

🔌 For Proxy Companion use

No installation required — run companion.exe directly to capture Postman traffic in the Builder.

📊 For Newman + Allure DDT use

These tools are needed to run DDT test suites from the command line and generate Allure reports. Install after Node.js.

Step 1 — Node.js
1
Install Node.js
nodejs.org

Download and run the LTS installer (v18 or newer) from:

https://nodejs.org/en/download

Run the .msi installer keeping all default options. It adds both node and npm to your PATH automatically.

💡 Leave "Add to PATH" checked during installation — required for commands in the next steps to work.

After the installer finishes, open a new PowerShell window and verify:

node --version
# v18.x.x or higher
npm --version
# 9.x.x or higher
PS C:\> node --version
v22.3.0
PS C:\> npm --version
10.8.1
Step 2 — Newman
2
Install Newman globally
npm global

Newman is the CLI runner for Postman collections. Install it globally so it is available from any folder:

npm install -g newman

Verify:

PS C:\> newman --version
6.2.1
Step 3 — Allure CLI
3
Install Allure command-line tool
npm global

Allure CLI generates the interactive HTML report from the allure-results folder produced by the reporter.

For full installation instructions and prerequisites see the official docs:

https://allurereport.org/docs/v2/install/
Step 4 — Custom DDT Reporter
4
Install newman-reporter-allure-ddt from local folder
npm local

The custom reporter is distributed as a local package folder. Navigate to the folder containing the reporter's package.json and install it globally:

# go to the reporter source folder
cd C:\path\to\newman-reporter-allure-ddt
# install from current directory
npm install -g .

💡 The dot . means "current directory". You can also pass the full path directly: npm install -g C:\path\to\newman-reporter-allure-ddt

Verify Newman can see the reporter:

npm list -g --depth=0 | findstr allure-ddt
PS C:\> npm list -g --depth=0 | findstr allure-ddt
+-- newman-reporter-allure-ddt@1.0.0
4b
Update the reporter after source changes
optional
🔄

Optional — only needed when the reporter source code is updated. Re-run the same install command from the reporter folder — npm replaces the previous version in-place. No uninstall step required.

cd C:\path\to\newman-reporter-allure-ddt
npm install -g .
Step 5 — Verify & Run
5
Verify everything and run Newman
Done ✓

Run these four commands — all should return a version number without errors:

node --version
v22.3.0
newman --version
6.2.1
allure --version
2.32.0
npm list -g --depth=0 | findstr allure-ddt
+-- newman-reporter-allure-ddt@1.0.0

Run Newman with the DDT reporter:

newman run collection.json \
  -d data.json \
  -e env.postman_environment.json \
  -r allure-ddt
💡

Serve the Allure report after the run (auto-opens in browser):
allure serve allure-results

📋 Step-by-Step Guide

Using TestReport Builder

From launching the app to generating your first HTML test report — step by step.

1
Overview of the interface
Builder UI

TestReport Builder opens with a two-panel layout. The top navbar holds all file and report actions. The left sidebar manages your test cases list.

2
Open the Proxy capture panel
Proxy

Click the Proxy button in the sidebar toolbar. The button indicator turns blue and the proxy panel slides in below.

3
Connect to the Companion
Proxy

Ensure companion.exe is running. Enter the port (default 8889), then click Connect to start the SSE capture session.

4
Capture traffic via Postman
Proxy

Once Connected — listening is shown (green dot), run your Postman requests. Each intercepted call is automatically added as a new test case in the sidebar.

5
Review and fill in test cases
Builder UI

Click a test case in the sidebar to open its editor. Fill in: Test Case Name, Method (GET/POST/…), API Path, App Name. Under Test Result set the Result (Passed / Failed / Skipped) and the Actual Status Code. Optionally attach a Dynatrace Log File and fill the Request Payload. When all cases are ready, click Generate Report.

6
Generate the HTML report
Output

The generated report opens as a standalone HTML file. The Dashboard tab shows Total / Passed / Failed / Skipped counters and a summary table of all test cases with their method, API path, expected and actual status, and attached log file.

7
Explore the Detailed View
Output

Select a test from the list. The Details tab shows expected vs actual status codes and the full API response. Request body is displayed if present. The Logs tab shows attached Dynatrace logs. The Execution Comments tab can contain free-form comments and screenshots added during test execution.

8
Open Git Settings
Builder → Settings

Click the ⚙ Settings button in the top-right toolbar. The Settings modal opens on the Git tab, where you configure the GitHub repository that stores your test artifacts.

The Git tab contains three sections: Repository (GitHub connection), Root Folders (paths inside the repo), and HTML Viewer (link to Report Viewer).

9
Configure the repository connection
Builder → Settings → Git tab

Fill in the connection fields:

  • a
    Repository — owner/repo-name (e.g. myorg/test-reports)
  • b
    Branch — target branch, usually main
  • c
    Cloudflare Worker URL — your deployed proxy Worker; click Save after pasting it
  • d
    Click Sign in with GitHub — follow the device-flow prompt to authorize in the browser

💡 The Cloudflare Worker acts as an auth proxy so your GitHub token is never exposed to the browser. Deploy the provided worker.js once per team.

10
Configure project paths (Project tab)
Builder → Settings → Project tab

Switch to the Project tab to set per-project paths and metadata that are embedded into each artifact at publish time.

Key fields: App Name, Report Viewer URL (published in the generated report), Auth Skip Hosts (identity provider domains excluded from proxy interception, e.g. login.microsoftonline.com).

11
Publish artifacts with Git Sync
Builder → ↑ Git Sync

After generating the report and saving your execution file, click ↑ Git Sync in the toolbar. Select which artifacts to commit:

  • a
    Report — the generated HTML test report
  • b
    Execution — the saved execution JSON with comments and screenshots
  • c
    Dataset — the test case dataset JSON

Enter a commit message, then click Sync to Git. The files are committed and pushed to your repository branch.

12
Open an execution from Git
Builder → Open → From Git
Optional

To reload a previously saved execution from GitHub, click the arrow next to Open and choose Open from Git. Browse the Executions or Datasets tab and select a file to load it directly into the builder.

📂 Report Viewer Guide

Using Report Viewer

Browse, filter, and view published test reports stored in your GitHub repository — step by step.

1
Interface overview
Report Viewer

Open Report Viewer/report-viewer.html in Chrome or Edge. On first launch the sidebar shows “Not configured” and the main area shows “No report selected”.

The interface has three main areas: Topbar (repo indicator, Sync, Settings), Sidebar (search, filters, file tree), and Main panel (iframe renders the selected report).

2
Open Settings
Viewer → ⚙ Settings

Click the ⚙ Settings button (top-right). The Settings modal requires four things before you can connect:

  • a
    Repository — owner/repo-name
  • b
    Branch — e.g. main
  • c
    Cloudflare Worker URL — same Worker used in Builder; click Save after entering
  • d
    Sign in with GitHub — device-flow auth in the browser
3
Configure and sign in
Viewer → Settings → Save & connect

Fill in your repository details and paste the Cloudflare Worker URL, then click Save. After signing in with GitHub the button turns green showing your username. Click Save & connect to start the initial sync.

💡 Local Cache Folder — choose a local directory where report files are stored offline. Chrome/Edge 86+ required for the File System Access API. Once set, future visits load instantly from cache.

4
Browse the report tree
Viewer → Sidebar

After a successful sync the sidebar displays a folder tree of all reports in your repository under reports/. Each file shows a PASS / FAIL / SKIP status badge. Click any file to load it.

Use the Search box at the top to filter by filename or tag. The Viewer also re-syncs in the background every 5 minutes to pick up new reports automatically.

5
Filter reports
Viewer → Filters

Click ▼ Filters to open the filter panel. You can narrow reports by:

  • a
    Apps — application tags from the report metadata
  • b
    APIs — API path tags (e.g. /customers/{id})
  • c
    Executors — the author name stored in the report
  • d
    Date — execution date from the report header

Click Apply to apply the active filters. The sidebar tree updates instantly to show only matching reports.

6
View a report
Viewer → Main panel

Click any file in the tree. The report opens in the main panel on the right — a full standalone HTML test report rendered inside an iframe. The selected file is highlighted in the tree.

  • •
    Use the ↔ Split / Full button in the topbar to hide the sidebar for more reading space.
  • •
    The Sync button forces an immediate refresh from GitHub.
  • •
    The ☽ / ☀ button toggles dark/light theme.
☁️ GitHub OAuth Proxy

GitHub OAuth Proxy — Cloudflare Worker deployment

Deploy cloudflare-worker.js: OAuth Device Flow backend with D1 session storage. The GitHub token never reaches the browser.

1
GitHub App
2
Worker
3
Secrets
4
D1 DB
☁️ Cloudflare Free Plan — limits
Workers
  • • 100 000 requests/day
  • • 10 ms CPU per request
  • • 128 MB memory
  • • 1 MB script size (compressed)
D1 Database
  • • 5 GB storage
  • • 5M rows read/day
  • • 100K rows write/day
  • • 10 databases max

For a single-project OAuth proxy these limits are more than sufficient. Workers Paid plan: $5/mo — limits ×10.

1
GitHub OAuth App
GitHub

The Worker uses GitHub OAuth Device Flow — requires an OAuth App (not a GitHub App). Client Secret is not needed — Device Flow works as a public client.

  • 1
    Navigate to: github.com → Settings → Developer settings → OAuth Apps → New OAuth App.
  • 2
    Fill in: Application name (any), Homepage URL (any), Authorization callback URL (any — Device Flow does not use a redirect).
  • 3
    Check Enable Device Flow — without it authorization will not work.
  • 4
    Click Register application.
  • 5
    Copy the Client ID (a string like Ov23li...) — you will need it in step 3 as the secret GITHUB_CLIENT_ID.
github.com → Settings → Developer settings → OAuth Apps → New OAuth App → ☑ Enable Device Flow → Register
2
Deploy Worker
Dashboard

Open dash.cloudflare.com → select your account → Workers & Pages.

  • 1
    Click Create → tab Workers → Create Worker.
  • 2
    Enter the worker name (e.g. github-oauth-worker); the URL will be <name>.<subdomain>.workers.dev.
  • 3
    Click Deploy — a worker is created with stub code.
  • 4
    Click Edit Code — the Quick Editor opens.
  • 5
    Replace all stub code with the contents of cloudflare-worker.js from the repository.
  • 6
    Click Save and deploy. ⚠ The worker will fail with an error until secrets and D1 are added — this is expected.
Workers & Pages → Create → Workers → Create Worker → Deploy → Edit Code → Paste code → Save and deploy
3
Secrets (4 items)
Dashboard

Workers & Pages → your worker → Settings → section Variables and Secrets → Add.

All four are type Secret (encrypted; values are hidden after saving).

GITHUB_CLIENT_ID Secret

Client ID from step 1 (OAuth App). Example: Ov23liXXXXXXXXXXXXXX

GITHUB_SCOPE Secret

OAuth scope. For private repos: repo. Public repos only: public_repo.

ENCRYPTION_KEY Secret

Base64-encoded 32-byte AES-GCM key for encrypting tokens in D1. Generate once in the terminal and copy the value:

openssl rand -base64 32

The result is a string like xK3...=. Paste it as the secret value. Keep a copy in a safe place — if lost, all sessions will need to be recreated.

ALLOWED_ORIGIN Secret

Allowed origins, comma-separated. Choose your scenario:

GitHub Pages
https://username.github.io

Replace username with your GitHub login. If the site is in a subfolder: https://username.github.io/repo-name — the origin is still without a path, just scheme + domain.

Local server (npm run dev)
http://localhost:8000

The port depends on your server. For a server on 8080 — http://localhost:8080.

Opening an HTML file directly (file://)
null

The browser sends Origin: null for file:// pages. Add the string null to the list.

Multiple origins at once
https://username.github.io,http://localhost:8000,null

Values are comma-separated with no spaces. The Worker allows each of the listed origins.

⚠ After adding each secret — click Save and deploy. Without the D1 binding the Worker will still fail — this is normal.
4
D1 Database
Storage

4a. Create the database

  • 1
    Storage & Databases → D1 SQL Database → Create database.
  • 2
    Name: github-oauth-db (matches the comment in the Worker — for readability; technically any name works).
  • 3
    Click Create.

4b. Create tables via Console

  • 1
    Open the created database → tab Console.
  • 2
    Paste the full SQL from the Worker, click Execute.
-- auth_polling: stores device_code for the duration of Device Flow (TTL 900s)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS auth_polling (
  session_id  TEXT    PRIMARY KEY,
  device_code TEXT    NOT NULL,
  interval    INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 5,
  created_at  INTEGER NOT NULL
);

-- user_sessions: stores the encrypted token (TTL 28800s = 8h)
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS user_sessions (
  session_id      TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  encrypted_token TEXT NOT NULL,
  iv              TEXT NOT NULL,
  login           TEXT NOT NULL,
  created_at      INTEGER NOT NULL
);

CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_ap_ts ON auth_polling(created_at);
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_us_ts ON user_sessions(created_at);

4c. Bind D1 to the Worker

  • 1
    Workers & Pages → your worker → Settings → Bindings → Add → D1 Database.
  • 2
    Variable name: DB (exactly as used in the Worker code — env.DB).
  • 3
    Select github-oauth-db from the list.
  • 4
    Click Save and deploy — the Worker is now fully ready.
Settings → Bindings → Add → D1 Database → Variable: DB → github-oauth-db → Save and deploy

Worker endpoints

POST /auth/start Starts Device Flow → returns session_id, user_code, verification_uri
GET /auth/poll?sid= Browser polls for status. Statuses: pending / ok / denied / expired
POST /auth/signout Deletes the session from D1 (header X-Session-ID)
GET /api/github/* GitHub API proxy — the browser sends X-Session-ID, the Worker injects the token
✔ The GitHub token never leaves the Worker. The browser receives only a session_id (UUID) — passed via the X-Session-ID header. Sessions expire automatically: polling 15 min, user session 8 h. Stale records are cleaned up on every POST /auth/start.
